title: Trade and International Business: Research & Analysis | CSIS
text:
Trade and International Business

Reciprocal tariffs, threats to global supply chains, and comparative manufacturing advantages have disrupted the established global trade system. CSIS scholars provide analysis and commentary on the international trading system and its institutions.

Image

Photo: Kevin Carter/Getty Images

Energy Considerations for EXIM Reauthorization

Rejuvenating EXIM will be crucial to U.S. competitiveness as export credit agencies play an increasingly important role in global energy finance.

Three Nations, One Table: North American Agriculture under USMCA

Join the Americas Program for a conference on USMCA and the future of North American food and agriculture. Experts will discuss food affordability, the successes and challenges of agricultural integration, and the risks that broader trade tensions pose to the agreement’s agricultural framework ahead of the 2026 review.

Event

— June 3, 2026

Why the West Keeps Losing Critical Mineral Assets to China

China is winning the race for critical minerals long before mines enter production. As Chinese firms acquire strategic mineral assets worldwide, the United States and its allies need new tools to identify, protect, and compete for ownership of future supply.

Commentary
by
Gracelin Baskaran

— June 2, 2026

Economic Warfare and Military Power

As strategic competition intensifies, the United States needs a clearer way to use finance, trade, industrial policy, and regulation to strengthen its own defense base while making it harder for adversaries to turn economic strength into military power.

by

Benjamin Jensen
and
Jacob Clayton
